skill.name: gif-sticker-maker
skill.description: Creates four animated GIF stickers from user photos using MiniMax image and video generation, localized captions, and ffmpeg conversion.
skill.summary: Create animated GIF sticker packs from photos with captions.
skill.author: MiniMax-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found command execution, network, filesystem, and credential-access patterns. Review confirms these patterns support the advertised MiniMax generation workflow, but they create privacy and local-file risks because user media is uploaded and output paths are user controlled. No prompt injection attempt or confirmed malicious behavior was found.

medium_findings.title: User Media Sent to External MiniMax API
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The image and video scripts send prompts, optional subject-reference images, and generated-media requests to the configured MiniMax API endpoint with bearer authentication. This matches the skill purpose, but users should understand that photos and prompts leave the local environment.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network calls and local image encoding are directly visible, and they are semantically required for MiniMax generation. The risk is privacy exposure rather than confirmed credential theft.

medium_findings.title: Environment-Controlled API Endpoint Receives API Key
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The scripts read MINIMAX_API_KEY and MINIMAX_API_BASE from the environment, then attach the key as an Authorization header. This is normal API-client behavior, but an unsafe MINIMAX_API_BASE value could send the key to an unintended endpoint.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code clearly combines an environment-sourced base URL with a bearer token header. Legitimate configuration is likely, but the endpoint is not constrained in code.

medium_findings.title: Arbitrary Local Path Reads and Writes
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The scripts read user-specified image paths, write generated images and videos to user-specified output paths, and remove a generated palette file. This is expected for media tooling, but it can overwrite files or read sensitive files if invoked with unsafe paths.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file operations are explicit and driven by command-line arguments. They are normal for this skill, but no path allowlist or workspace restriction is present.

low_findings.title: ffmpeg Subprocess Invocation Has Limited Injection Surface
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The converter invokes ffmpeg through subprocess.run with an argument list and shell is not enabled. This still executes an external binary, but the reviewed code does not concatenate user input into a shell command.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess calls use list arguments and do not set shell=True. The remaining risk is dependency trust and processing untrusted media, not direct shell injection.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command Examples Are Documentation
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Static analysis flagged shell-style backticks in SKILL.md, but these are documented command examples and XML output examples for the assistant workflow. They are not executable code inside SKILL.md.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are fenced Markdown examples. They do instruct the assistant to run local scripts during normal use, but SKILL.md itself is not executing Ruby or shell backticks.

low_findings.title: Weak-Crypto and Obfuscation Matches Are Benign Media Handling
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The reported weak cryptography locations do not implement hashing or encryption. Base64 encode/decode is used to send image data to the API or decode image responses, which is expected media serialization rather than obfuscation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines contain argument parsing, Markdown text, and base64 media conversion. No evidence of weak cryptographic primitives or hidden payload decoding was found.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Matches Are Status and Error Reporting
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The video script prints task IDs, model settings, prompts, and file IDs returned by the MiniMax API. This is operational status output, not host reconnaissance or environment enumeration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The data printed comes from the API workflow and user inputs. I did not find evidence of collecting hostnames, usernames, process lists, or other system inventory.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bearer Token Network Requests
dangerous_patterns.description: The scripts send Authorization bearer headers to an environment-selected API base URL. Use only trusted MiniMax endpoints and avoid logging tokens.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code directly constructs Authorization headers and performs requests. This is expected but sensitive API-client behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Image Encoding for Remote Generation
dangerous_patterns.description: The scripts read local image files and encode them as base64 data URIs for image or video generation. Users should avoid passing private or unintended files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The local read and base64 conversion are explicit. The privacy impact depends on which paths the user or agent provides.

dangerous_patterns.title: External ffmpeg Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The GIF converter runs ffmpeg to process MP4 inputs. The implementation avoids shell concatenation, but processing untrusted media through external codecs still has dependency risk.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code clearly invokes ffmpeg as an external executable. The main residual concern is codec and binary trust, not shell command injection.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Controlled Output File Writes
dangerous_patterns.description: Output paths are accepted from command-line arguments and written directly. Run the skill in a workspace and avoid privileged paths.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The scripts create directories and write files based on CLI arguments. This is required for media output but lacks path restriction.

content.user_title: Create Animated GIF Stickers from Photos
content.value_statement: Making a polished sticker pack from a photo requires image generation, animation, captions, and file conversion. This skill coordinates MiniMax generation and ffmpeg conversion to produce four captioned GIF stickers.
actual_capabilities: Creates four sticker concepts from a user photo with matching captions.
Generates square static sticker images through the MiniMax image API.
Animates each sticker image through the MiniMax video API.
Converts generated MP4 videos into GIF files with ffmpeg.
Supports localized default captions from the included caption workflow.
limitations: Requires MINIMAX_API_KEY, MINIMAX_API_BASE, Python dependencies, and ffmpeg.
Sends prompts and selected media files to the configured MiniMax API endpoint.
Subject reference matching is documented for person subjects only.
Does not restrict input or output paths to a specific workspace.

use_cases.title: Create Social Sticker Packs
use_cases.description: Turn a profile photo into four expressive GIF stickers for chat apps, posts, or community updates.

use_cases.title: Prototype Branded Reactions
use_cases.description: Generate quick animated reactions from a mascot, product object, or logo-inspired prompt for campaign review.

use_cases.title: Make Personalized Avatars
use_cases.description: Create a small animated avatar set with consistent captions and actions for personal or team use.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Sticker Pack
prompt_templates.prompt: Create four animated GIF stickers from this photo. Use the default captions for my language.

prompt_templates.title: Custom Caption Pack
prompt_templates.prompt: Create four animated GIF stickers from this photo with these captions: Hi, Haha, Sorry, Love.

prompt_templates.title: Brand Mascot Reactions
prompt_templates.scenario: Use an object or logo as the visual reference.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a four-sticker reaction pack from this mascot image. Use cheerful actions and short English captions.

prompt_templates.title: Localized Character Set
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request with language, tone, and consistent output needs.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create four GIF stickers from this portrait. Keep the same figurine style, use Spanish captions, and make the actions friendly and expressive.

output_examples.input: A user provides a portrait and asks for default captioned stickers.
output_examples.output: Four GIF stickers are delivered: a waving sticker, a laughing sticker, a crying sticker, and a heart gesture sticker.

output_examples.input: A user provides a pet photo and four custom captions.
output_examples.output: A matching animated sticker set is created with the supplied captions and pet-inspired actions.

output_examples.input: A team provides a mascot image for a reaction pack.
output_examples.output: A square animated GIF pack is produced with consistent styling and campaign-ready reaction poses.

best_practices: Use clear, high-resolution source images with one main subject.
Keep captions between one and three words for readable sticker text.
Run the scripts in a dedicated workspace with trusted API endpoint settings.
anti_patterns: Do not use private photos unless users accept third-party API processing.
Do not send unrelated local files as subject references or first-frame images.
Do not write outputs to system directories or shared sensitive folders.

faq.question: What does this skill create?
faq.answer: It creates four animated GIF stickers from a photo, with captions and expressive actions.

faq.question: Does it need an external service?
faq.answer: Yes. It uses the configured MiniMax API endpoint for image and video generation.

faq.question: What credentials are required?
faq.answer: It requires MINIMAX_API_KEY and MINIMAX_API_BASE in the environment before generation starts.

faq.question: Can I customize the captions?
faq.answer: Yes. You can provide four short captions, or use the default localized captions.

faq.question: Can it preserve a person’s likeness?
faq.answer: The workflow supports subject references for person subjects through the MiniMax image script.

faq.question: What should users review before publishing?
faq.answer: Users should review privacy requirements, API endpoint settings, output paths, and rights to use the source image.
